schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads the enlarged bronze altar (twenty cubits square, against Moses' five) as fit to the enlarged worship of a greater people, and its burning sacrifice as a lead to the one great Sacrifice — the propitiation faith must keep in view.
Clarke lays out the chapter's frame — altar, sea, lavers, candlesticks, tables, basins, courts, and all the vessels of gold and bronze for the house of God.

themes: the altar as type of Christ's sacrifice

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch treat the divergences from 1 Kings 7:23 (bekarim/pekaim, 3000 vs 2000 baths) as orthographical slips, not real differences; Ellicott shows how the chronicler could substitute a commoner word of like sense for a rare one.
Henry reads the molten sea, set like a font at the entrance to the priests' court, as a summons to purity at the threshold of worship.

themes: faithful copying of an older record

schools.summary: Ellicott defends the chronicler's 'oxen' as an authentic ornament that need not be corrected from Kings, answering Reuss with Assyrian parallels; Keil takes it for one of the orthographical slips to be restored from the older text.
Clarke leans to correction — 'oxen' (bekarim) a corruption of 'gourds/grapes' (pekaim) — reporting but rejecting Houbigant's defence of both readings.

debates.question: Are the 'oxen' round the sea's brim authentic, or a scribal corruption of the 'wild gourds' of 1 Kings 7:24?

sides.position: a copyist's corruption of 'gourds' into 'oxen,' to be restored from the older text
an authentic ornament; both oxen and gourds could have been present, and it is dogmatism to emend
both texts are right, the word meaning a large kind of grape (Houbigant, as reported by Clarke)

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the verse coincides verbally with 1 Kings 7:25 but for a rare spelling of 'twelve' — a mark of close copying; Keil sets the twelve oxen with their faces to the four quarters, bearing the sea.
Ellicott and Keil lay the 3000/2000 discrepancy at a copyist's door — the unpointed forms of 'thousands' and 'two thousand' confused — and defend the pleonastic 'it held' against the charge of gloss.
Clarke takes the difference as real, explained by a smaller post-exilic (Babylonish) bath, or by the Targum's dry-measure/liquid-measure distinction.
Henry reads the vast capacity as a sign of the gospel privilege that Christ's blood cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7) — a fountain opened wide as the sea.

debates.question: Why does the sea hold 3,000 baths here where 1 Kings 7:26 gives 2,000?

sides.position: a textual corruption; the unpointed forms of the numeral were confused in copying
a real figure, explained by a smaller post-exilic bath, or the Targum's dry/liquid distinction

themes: textual corruption or a smaller measure

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses the word 'laver' as a washing-pan and notes the chronicler's abbreviating omission of the ten wheeled stands of 1 Kings 7:27; Keil identifies the lavers as those on the brazen wheeled chests of 1 Kings 7:38.
Clarke distinguishes the uses: the lavers rinsed the parts of the victims, while the sea served the priests' own washing and bathing.
Henry reads the two washings as a double lesson — put away the vain thoughts that cleave to our very performances (lavers), and renew repentance whenever we minister (the sea, James 4:8; John 13:10).

themes: cleansing the worshipper and the worship

schools.summary: Keil notes these verses have no counterpart in 1 Kings 7 and were likely dropped from it; Ellicott renders 'according to their form' as 'their prescribed rule' (cf. Exodus 25:31) and places the lamps before the chancel.
Henry reads the growth from one candlestick to ten as the swelling canon of Scripture and the multiplied churches of the gospel temple (Revelation 1:20).
Wesley: 'their form' is the old form God prescribed to Moses — the new lamps shaped after the ancient pattern.

themes: growth of the canon and the churches

schools.summary: Keil corrects two guesses — the tables held shewbread, not the lamps, and the basins were wine-flagons for libations, not blood-vessels (Amos 6:6); Ellicott notes the chronicler's plural 'tables' against the one table of 1 Kings 7:48.
Clarke takes the basins for paterae used in libations; Wesley marks the twelve loaves per table and the lesson that 'as the house was enlarged, so was the provision.'
Henry reads the enlarged table and its hundred basins as the abundance of the Father's house — 'bread enough for the whole family' — a shadow of the gospel provision.

themes: ten tables and a hundred basins

schools.summary: Ellicott shows 'the great court' is a late Targumic word for the outer court omitted in Kings, and illustrates the bronze-plated doors from Shalmaneser's palace at Balawat; Keil marks the copper leaves and threads on to the altar-vessels.
Clarke distinguishes the inner court of the priests from the outer great court where the people assembled.
Henry reads the brass-plated doors as strength and beauty against the weather, recalling the 'gates of brass' of Psalms 107:16.

themes: the court of the priests and the great court
the outer court omitted by Kings
strength of the house of God

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the placement literally ('on the right shoulder, eastward') as the south-east side, agreeing with 1 Kings 7:39, and notes the LXX's lost 'of the house'; Keil treats the half-verse as the seam turning to the summary catalogue of Huram's bronze.
Henry praises the craftsman who 'finished all' (the honoured 'Father Huram') and the king whose vessels 'in great abundance' were all devoted — 'freely he has received, and he will freely give.'
Ellicott prefers the chronicler's 'pots' (ash-scuttles) to the older 'lavers,' identifies the 'basons' as bowls, and renders the clause simply 'he made.'

Ellicott clears the archaic English — 'pommels' and 'chapiters' are globes and capitals, 'wreaths' are lattices (network, 1 Kings 7:41) — and parses the feminine 'two'; Keil gathers the whole inventory of Huram's brass cast in the plain of Jordan.

themes: the two pillars and their capitals
cast in the plain of Jordan

schools.summary: Ellicott simply refers the four hundred pomegranates in their two rows to the parallel count of 1 Kings 7:42 — where chronicler and source agree exactly, there is nothing to correct.

Ellicott reads the suspicious doubled verb 'he made... made he' as an ancient corruption (already in the LXX) of 'the bases ten, and the lavers ten,' the Hebrew for 'ten' resembling 'he made'; the elaborate stands of 1 Kings 7:27 the chronicler has abridged.
Ellicott notes the chronicler's abridged wording — 'the sea one,' 'the oxen twelve under it' — clipping the fuller phrases of the older account for a swifter inventory.

themes: the sea and its twelve oxen

schools.summary: Ellicott suspects 'fleshhooks' should be 'bowls' (though both may belong, cf. Exodus 27:3), fixes 'bright' as 'polished' (Jeremiah 46:4), and denies 'their instruments' means the moulds.
Clarke and Wesley agree 'his father' is the Hebrew 'ab used for a master or chief craftsman — a title of honour Solomon gave the Tyrian artificer, not a claim of kinship.

themes: 'his father' as a title of a master-craftsman

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'clay ground' as 'the thickness of the ground' (stiff soil, cf. Job 15:26) and identifies Zeredathah with the Zarethan of Joshua 3:16 and Zeredah of 1 Kings 11:26.
Clarke reports the view that the clay, ideal for moulds, may have been brought from the Jordan plain rather than the vessels cast there.

themes: the foundry in the Jordan plain

schools.summary: Ellicott compares the chronicler's 'made all these vessels in great abundance' with the older 'left all the vessels unweighed,' tracing the change to a copyist or to the chronicler easing a difficult verb; the weight 'was not ascertained.'

Keil marks the stressed use of the lamps and the divergence over the shewbread tables; Ellicott explains the chronicler's plural 'tables' as a grouping of several sources with the one table of the Presence (cf. Ezekiel 40:39).
Henry reads the golden altar of incense as Christ, who having made atonement 'ever lives, making intercession' — the inner altar answering the outer altar of propitiation.

themes: the golden altar and the tables
the altar of incense as Christ's intercession

schools.summary: Keil notes the snuffers/knives, absent from the tabernacle but present in the later temple inventories (2 Kings 12:14; Jeremiah 52:18); Ellicott marks 'that they should burn after the manner' as the chronicler's addition, the number already given earlier.
Wesley glosses 'the manner' as 'according to the prescription of God to Moses' — the lamps burning by the old rule.

themes: the lamps burning before the oracle

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'that perfect gold' as 'perfection of gold' (a word found nowhere else), names the snuffers, trays, and snuff-dishes, and defends the chronicler's 'entry' against emendation to the unique 'hinges' of the older text.
Clarke pictures each branch of the lampstand wrought like a plant in flower, the flower's opening being the lamp or its support.

themes: flowers, lamps, and tongs of gold

schools.summary: Clarke takes the golden doors as overlaid with plates and grants that the temple was typical in its general design, yet cautions that minute spiritualizing of every utensil is 'weak, silly, religious trifling,' ill fitted to honour revelation.

outliers.note: Sets a caution against the chapter's exuberant typology: grant the general gospel reference of the temple, but do not fasten a doctrine on each knop, flower, and snuffer — such minute spiritualizing cheapens the Scripture it means to honour.

themes: the golden doors of the most holy place
the temple typical in its general design
the account of the furniture closed
